quality.rationale: 基于微分同胚不变性与作用量变分的严格推导，物理逻辑严密且可推广至任意广义协变场论。

origin.question:
Problem 21.3. If $\mathbf{L}$ is a Lagrangian density as in Problem 21.2, the stress-energy tensor can be defined by a variation of $\mathbf{g}_{\mu 
u}$ in $S$ :
$$
\delta S = \intop \frac {\delta (\mathrm {L} (- \mathrm {g})^{\frac {1}{2}})}{\delta \mathbf {g}_{\mu \nu}} \delta \mathbf {g}_{\mu \nu} \mathrm {d}^{4} \mathbf {x} \equiv \frac {1}{2} \intop \mathrm {T}^{\mu \nu} \delta \mathbf {g}_{\mu \nu} (- \mathbf {g})^{\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d}^{4} \mathbf {x} .
$$
Show that $\mathbf{T}^{\mu \nu}_{;\nu} = 0$ follows from the equation of motion of the field and the fact that S is a scalar.
origin.solution:
Solution 21.3. Since $\mathbf{L}(-\mathbf{g})^{ rac{1}{2}}\mathbf{d}^{4}\mathbf{x}$ is a scalar, it is unchanged under the infinitesimal coordinate transformation
$$
\overline {{\mathbf {x}}}^{\mu} = \mathbf {x}^{\mu} + \xi^{\mu}.
$$
By renaming the dummy variable of integration, $\sf{d}^4\overline{\mathbf{x}}$ can be changed back to $\sf{d}^4\mathbf{x}$ . The net change in $\mathbf{L}(-\mathbf{g})^{ rac{1}{2}}$ is therefore the Lie derivative (see Problem 8.17), so that
$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \delta S = \int \mathcal {L}_{\xi} (L (- g)^{\frac {1}{2}}) d^{4} x \\
= \int \left[ \frac {\delta \underline {{\mathrm {L}}}}{\delta \Phi^{\mathrm {A}}} \left(\mathcal {Q}_{\xi} \Phi^{\mathrm {A}}\right) (- \mathrm {g})^{\frac {1}{2}} + \frac {\delta (\mathrm {L} (- \mathrm {g})^{\frac {1}{2}})}{\delta \mathrm {g}_{\mu \nu}} \mathcal {Q}_{\xi} \mathrm {g}_{\mu \nu} \right] d^{4} x. \tag {1} \\
\end{array}
$$
The first term in the integrand vanishes by the equation of motion (Problem 21.2), thus
$$
0 = \frac {1}{2} \int \mathrm {T}^{\mu \nu} \mathcal {L}_{\pmb {\xi}} \mathbf {g}_{\mu \nu} (- \mathbf {g})^{\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d}^{4} \mathbf {x} .
$$
Since
$$
\mathcal {L}_{\xi} \mathrm {g}_{\mu \nu} = \xi_{\mu ; \nu} + \xi_{\nu ; \mu}
$$
we get
$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \int \mathrm {T}^{\mu \nu} \xi_{\mu ; \nu} (- \mathrm {g})^{\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d}^{4} \mathrm {x} \tag {2} \\
= \int \left(\mathrm {T}^{\mu \nu} \xi_{\mu}\right)_{; \nu} (- \mathrm {g})^{\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d}^{4} \mathrm {x} - \int \mathrm {T}_{; \nu}^{\mu \nu} \xi_{\mu} (- \mathrm {g})^{\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d}^{4} \mathrm {x}. \\
\end{array}.
$$
The first term is
$$
\int \left(\mathrm {T}^{\mu \nu} \xi_{\mu}\right)_{; \nu} (- \mathrm {g})^{\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d}^{4} \mathrm {x} = \int \left((- \mathrm {g})^{\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {T}^{\mu \nu} \xi_{\mu}\right)_{, \nu} \mathrm {d}^{4} \mathrm {x}
$$
and gives a surface integral which vanishes. Since $\xi_{\mu}$ is arbitrary,
Equation (2) then gives
$$
\mathrm {T}_{; \nu}^{\mu \nu} = 0.
$$
